author: Macmill-340
text:
YOUR FIRST PROGRAM
Every programming language has a tradition: the first program you write prints 'Hello, World!' to the screen. Python makes this as simple as it can be.

THE print() FUNCTION
A function is a built-in action you can call by writing its name followed by parentheses. Whatever you put inside the parentheses is the input.

print() displays something on the screen — it is how your program communicates with the user.

SYNTAX:
    print("Hello, World!")

Whatever you put inside the quotes is printed exactly as written. You can use single or double quotes:
    print('Hello, World!')     # also works

You can print anything as text:
    print("Python is running")
    print("42")

COMMON MISTAKES:
Forgetting the parentheses — not valid in Python 3:
    print "Hello, World!"     # SyntaxError
    print("Hello, World!")    # Correct

Forgetting the quotes — Python treats it as a variable name:
    print(Hello, World!)       # NameError
    print("Hello, World!")    # Correct
common_errors: Missing parentheses — `print "Hello"` causes a SyntaxError in Python 3. Always use `print("Hello")`.
task: Write a program that prints exactly: Hello, World!
